Chris Johnson
Plus
You said:
A friend of mine just sent me this career description: --- As a programmer, I sat in a chair for 15 years not moving, hunched over, and not talking to people. This severely fucked up my body and mind in ways that have taken 7 years to repair. I am now basically a world expert on managing the physical and mental health of programmers, software engineers, and related professions. I know a gigantic ton of basic physical therapy techniques. So; PT, occupational therapy, getting an OD*, breathwork, massage, body and energy work. These need to fit into some kind of formal system for me to practice them professionally. So; licensure, training, and— yeah, fuck school. I’m not going back to school. (So no OD*, probably.) Tai chi, qigong, CARs, meditation training, “mind-body wellness spas”, strength training, and — yeah, there’s an entire nutrition expertise model in here as well. Counseling is the other aspect — whatever is below “therapy” (which is a bunch of licensed bullshit, of the kind that I’m not interested in). I’m not a therapist. --- He was an elite, top-tier software engineer at major tech corporations once, but he has retired from his "first career" of working with people. He now wants a career where he can work with people, heal people's minds and bodies, work through messy memories and former relationships, practice and learn about the transformative power of energy work. What are the top 10-15 career paths he should consider pursuing? He is currently 35 years old, and doesn't have much money to go back to school. But list those options as well, if they seem especially relevant.

ChatGPT said:

I’ll map what your friend wants to do (hands-on bodywork + movement + “below therapy” coaching, ideally low-cost/low-school) into concrete, real job titles, and I’ll flag which ones require licensure vs short certifications vs longer schooling.

Here are 15 concrete career titles that match what he’s describing, sorted roughly from lowest-school / fastest to more formal / longer. I’m being blunt about licensure boundaries because it matters for how he can work with people without stepping into “medical treatment” or psychotherapy.

Low-school / fastest on-ramp (months, not years)

  1. Licensed Massage Therapist (LMT)

    • Why it fits: hands-on bodywork; clients who sit at desks; “repair the programmer body” niche is perfect.

    • Credential: state licensure (training hours + exam; varies by state).

    • What he can do: soft-tissue work, relaxation, mobility-adjacent work (within scope), referral network.

  2. Structural Integration Practitioner (e.g., “Rolfing”-style)

    • Why it fits: posture/compensation patterns from desk life; deep bodywork + movement integration vibe.

    • Credential: private-school certification (not usually state-licensed as its own thing; often paired with LMT).

    • Note: credibility tends to be higher if he’s also an LMT.

  3. Reiki Practitioner / Energy Healing Practitioner

    • Why it fits: explicitly matches the “energy work” and transformative-spiritual angle.

    • Credential: short trainings; not licensed medical care.

    • Reality check: market is real, but credibility varies; pairing with a mainstream credential (LMT, trainer) helps.

  4. Breathwork Facilitator

    • Why it fits: stress, panic-y somatic loops, performance anxiety, sleep—big overlap with programmer burnout.

    • Credential: certifications vary widely; choose a reputable program + strong ethics/screening.

    • Caution: can get intense; he’ll want clear boundaries and referral relationships.

  5. Meditation / Mindfulness Instructor

    • Why it fits: “mind-body” without being a therapist; also pairs well with breathwork and movement.

    • Credential: program-based teacher training (varies).

    • Angle: “practical contemplative training for engineers.”

  6. Tai Chi Instructor / Qigong Instructor

    • Why it fits: exactly his interest list; gentle movement + attention training + energetic framing.

    • Credential: instructor training through schools/lineages; not a license.

    • Best use: group classes + corporate sessions + private coaching.

  7. Feldenkrais Practitioner or Alexander Technique Teacher

    • Why it fits: postural habits, tension patterns, “relearning use” for desk-workers; deeply “mind-body.”

    • Credential: multi-year professional training (often part-time), but not a university degree.

    • Good for: long-term mastery + premium niche practice.

Coaching + movement (portable, relatively low cost)

  1. Certified Personal Trainer (CPT)

    • Why it fits: strength training + behavior change; easy entry; he can build “anti-desk” programming.

    • Credential: cert + insurance (not licensure).

    • Positioning: “strength + mobility for programmers” with objective assessments.

  2. Corrective Exercise Specialist (post-CPT add-on)

    • Why it fits: scratches the “basic PT techniques” itch without claiming PT.

    • Credential: certification.

    • Important boundary: he can coach movement and comfort, but not diagnose/treat injuries.

  3. Strength and Conditioning Coach (especially if he likes performance metrics)

  • Why it fits: more rigorous programming; credibility in the “elite engineer” demographic who respect skill.

  • Credential: cert(s); some paths prefer a degree, but private-sector reality varies.

  1. Ergonomics Specialist / Workplace Ergonomics Consultant

  • Why it fits: extremely aligned with desk-work harm; sells well to companies; measurable ROI.

  • Credential: certificates exist; deeper “human factors” degrees exist too (optional).

  • Business model: B2B assessments + workshops + follow-up coaching.

  1. Health Coach (e.g., board-certified style) / Lifestyle Coach

  • Why it fits: “below therapy” counseling: habits, sleep routines, stress management, nutrition basics.

  • Credential: certification; some tracks are more reputable and structured than others.

  • Boundary: coaching ≠ mental health treatment; he can still do meaningful work.

  1. Nutrition Coach (non-licensed)

  • Why it fits: he mentioned nutrition; helpful for energy, recovery, body composition, inflammation patterns.

  • Credential: certification; avoid medical nutrition therapy unless he becomes an RD.

  • Clean niche: “nutrition for cognitive performance + sedentary compensation.”

More formal options (more time/money, but relevant)

  1. Physical Therapist Assistant (PTA)

  • Why it fits: closest “formal system” to legitimately do rehab-adjacent work.

  • Schooling: typically an associate degree + clinicals + licensure.

  • Tradeoff: more school than he wants, but it’s the cleanest route into real PT practice.

  1. Occupational Therapy Assistant (OTA)

  • Why it fits: function-first, habits, daily life ergonomics, return-to-work; strong overlap with desk work and routines.

  • Schooling: typically an associate degree + licensure.

  • Often overlooked: can be a great match for “help people live better in their actual environments.”

Two practical “stacked” paths (if he wants maximum legitimacy with minimal school)

  • Path A: LMT → (Structural Integration or specialty bodywork) → Breathwork/Mindfulness/Tai Chi
    Hands-on + nervous system + energy work, with a mainstream license anchoring it.

  • Path B: CPT → Corrective Exercise → Ergonomics Consultant → Health Coach
    High compatibility with his engineer brain and corporate clients; scalable B2B.

If you tell me what state he’s in and whether he prefers 1:1 hands-on vs teaching groups vs corporate consulting, I can narrow this to the top ~5 that best match his constraints and temperament.


You said:
Excellent work! Now, do a wide search, and pull up some economics research on the U.S. market for me. What are the major career titles and industry sectors that overlap with this list? We're going to be building up both queries dynamically, so if you discover more career titles that answer the original query, list those as well.

ChatGPT said:

I’ll (1) web-browse for U.S. labor-market/economics research on wellness/bodywork/coaching/ergonomics and adjacent sectors, then (2) map that evidence to concrete job titles + industry sectors, adding any additional titles that expand your friend’s option set.

Below is what overlaps economically in the U.S. labor market—i.e., where employers spend money, where employment is projected to grow, and what formal job families match your friend’s “programmer-body + mind-body + coaching + ergonomics + energy work” cluster.

I’ll give you:

  1. the major industry sectors that intersect this work

  2. the career titles inside each sector (including new ones not in my first list)

  3. a few market signals from economics / labor research to anchor the map

Market signals (so we’re not vibing; we’re grounding)

  • Healthcare and social assistance is the dominant growth engine in BLS projections for 2024–2034. Bureau of Labor Statistics+1

  • Several directly relevant occupations are projected “much faster than average,” including:

    • Massage Therapists (+15% projected 2024–2034). Bureau of Labor Statistics

    • Fitness Trainers and Instructors (+12% projected 2024–2034). Bureau of Labor Statistics

    • Athletic Trainers (+11% projected 2024–2034). Bureau of Labor Statistics

    • Community Health Workers (+11% projected 2024–2034). Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • On the “corporate wellness” side, the best-known randomized trials find small behavior changes but no short-run reductions in spending/clinical markers (important for how he pitches B2B work). JAMA Network+2NBER+2

  • The economic burden of work-related musculoskeletal issues is large (lost productivity + compensation costs), which is a key justification for ergonomics / injury prevention roles. NCBI+2CDC Archive+2

1) Ambulatory healthcare and outpatient rehab

Sector: outpatient clinics, rehab centers, sports medicine, orthopedics, integrative medicine, pain clinics.
Why it overlaps: “fix bodies, teach movement, help function improve” is literally the product.

Major titles that overlap

  • Physical Therapist Assistant (PTA)

  • Occupational Therapy Assistant (OTA)

  • Athletic Trainer (often in clinics / schools / sports medicine) Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Rehabilitation Aide / PT Tech

  • Patient Care Coordinator / Care Coordinator (less hands-on bodywork, more “system navigation”)

  • Health Education Specialist (program delivery, prevention, patient education) Bureau of Labor Statistics

Notes on fit: PTA/OTA are the most “formal system” options, but they’re degree/licensure paths.

2) Personal services wellness

Sector: massage studios, spas, boutique wellness, independent practice, concierge wellness, men’s health clinics, “biohacker” studios.
Why it overlaps: this is where hands-on work + energy work + breath + somatics are most commercially native.

Major titles that overlap

  • Licensed Massage Therapist (LMT) Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Bodywork Practitioner (e.g., Structural Integration)

  • Reiki Practitioner / Energy Healing Practitioner

  • Breathwork Facilitator

  • Meditation / Mindfulness Instructor

  • Tai Chi Instructor / Qigong Instructor

  • Somatic Coach / Somatic Practitioner (unlicensed coaching lane; varies widely)

Market reality: This sector can be very viable, but income stability is often about positioning + retention + referrals.

3) Fitness and performance training

Sector: gyms, studios, sports performance, corporate gyms, online coaching, rehab-adjacent strength coaching.
Why it overlaps: “strength training + mobility + posture + stress” is a mainstream consumer category with strong projected growth.

Major titles that overlap

  • Fitness Trainer / Personal Trainer Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Strength and Conditioning Coach

  • Corrective Exercise Specialist (often an add-on cert, not a distinct BLS occupation)

  • Mobility Coach

  • Injury Prevention Specialist (common in athletic and industrial settings)

4) Workplace health, safety, and ergonomics (B2B)

Sector: occupational health, workers’ comp prevention, corporate safety, manufacturing/warehousing ergonomics, tech-company ergonomics, workstation/product ergonomics.
Why it overlaps: employers pay to reduce MSD risk and improve productivity; ergonomics is a recognized prevention lever. CDC+2OSHA+2

Major titles that overlap (and are highly aligned with “programmer-body” niche)

  • Ergonomics Specialist

  • Ergonomics Consultant

  • Human Factors Specialist / Human Factors Engineer (more formal/degree-leaning)

  • Environmental Health and Safety (EHS) Specialist (broader safety role; ergonomics can be a specialty)

  • Occupational Health Coordinator

  • Return-to-Work Coordinator / Disability Case Manager (often tied to HR/benefits/work comp)

  • Corporate Wellness Program Manager / Wellness Coordinator (but see wellness RCT evidence—pitch as engagement + retention + comfort, not cost savings) JAMA Network+2NBER+2

5) Public health and community-based prevention

Sector: hospitals’ community programs, nonprofits, public health departments, population health vendors.
Why it overlaps: this is where “wellness,” education, behavior change, and navigation live at scale.

Major titles that overlap

  • Community Health Worker Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Health Coach (program-based; often non-licensed)

  • Peer Support Specialist (usually mental-health oriented; training varies by state)

  • Health Education Specialist Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Wellness Navigator / Patient Navigator

6) Health insurance, benefits, and care management (less “hands-on,” more system leverage)

Sector: insurers, employer benefits, third-party wellness vendors, utilization management, care coordination companies.
Why it overlaps: this is where “behavior change + navigation + prevention programs” get funded and scaled.

Major titles that overlap

  • Health Coach (vendor/insurer setting)

  • Care Manager / Care Coordinator

  • Population Health Coordinator

  • Benefits Consultant (wellness/ergonomics programs)

  • Employee Assistance Program (EAP) Coordinator (note: EAP “counseling” roles can bump into licensure expectations)

Additional career titles that also fit the original ask (new additions)

These are “good matches” that didn’t appear in the first 15:

  • Corporate Wellness Program Manager / Wellness Coordinator JAMA Network+1

  • EHS Specialist (ergonomics focus) CDC+1

  • Return-to-Work Coordinator / Disability Case Manager NCBI

  • Human Factors Specialist / Human Factors Engineer OSHA

  • Rehabilitation Aide / PT Tech

  • Patient Navigator / Wellness Navigator

  • Injury Prevention Specialist (industry or athletics)

  • Somatic Practitioner / Somatic Coach (non-licensed; choose ethics + scope carefully)

One synthesis: the “overlap core” your friend is implicitly aiming at

If you zoom out, he’s targeting a blended professional identity that the market already recognizes in pieces:

“Ergonomics + movement coaching + hands-on bodywork + nervous-system skills”

The U.S. market expresses that as three main “paying customer” channels:

  1. Direct-to-consumer wellness (massage/bodywork + coaching + energy work) Bureau of Labor Statistics+1

  2. Clinical rehab ecosystem (assistant roles, athletic training, patient education) Bureau of Labor Statistics+1

  3. Employer spend (ergonomics/safety/return-to-work + select wellness programming) CDC+2OSHA+2

If you want, next we can turn this into two evolving query lists like:

  • Query A (career discovery): job titles + synonyms + credentials + settings

  • Query B (market sizing / economics): “employment projections,” “wages,” “industry size,” “workers’ comp,” “MSD costs,” “corporate wellness ROI,” etc.


You said:
Very, very good. Anything else to add, regarding the current two queries?

ChatGPT said:

Yep—two main upgrades will make your “dynamic queries” way more powerful: (a) add the official classification codes and synonyms so you catch how employers/researchers actually label this work, and (b) add the economics keywords/data sources that economists use (so you’re not stuck in blog-land).

Additions to Query A (career discovery)

1) Add “bridge” job families you haven’t explicitly pulled in yet

These are high-overlap with your friend’s intent, but often listed under different umbrellas:

  • Health & Wellness Program Coordinator / Health Promotion Specialist (often in hospitals, universities, large employers)

  • Occupational Health Technician (clinic + workplace health interface)

  • Workplace Injury Prevention Specialist (especially in logistics/warehousing; ergonomics-heavy)

  • Kinesiologist / Exercise Physiologist (more formal; sometimes clinical, sometimes performance)

  • Somatic Experiencing Practitioner (training-heavy but not a degree; sits in the “somatic trauma” lane without being psychotherapy—scope varies)

  • Yoga Therapist (distinct from yoga teacher; some programs are more structured; still not “therapy” licensure)

  • Pilates Instructor (Rehab-focused) (big overlap with posture/core/back pain populations)

  • Health Informatics adjacent: Digital Health Coach / Wellness Product Specialist (if he wants to stay near tech while working with people)

2) Add the “settings” filter (because titles vary wildly by setting)

Append to your title searches:

  • “outpatient clinic”, “integrative medicine”, “sports medicine”, “pain clinic”

  • “corporate wellness”, “occupational health”, “workers’ compensation”, “return to work”

  • “spa”, “concierge”, “private practice”, “wellness studio”

  • “university wellness”, “hospital community health”, “population health”

This catches the same work wearing different clothing.

3) Add credentials as search pivots (to map schooling vs not)

Even if he dislikes school, these are how the labor market is organized. Add as query terms:

  • “license”, “certification”, “scope of practice”, “state board”, “liability insurance”

  • “continuing education” + the modality (massage, ergonomics, breathwork, mindfulness)

4) Add the official classification hooks (critical for “wide search”)

Use these in searches because BLS, papers, and wage datasets key off them:

  • SOC (Standard Occupational Classification) terms: “SOC code massage therapist”, “SOC code fitness trainer”, “SOC code health educator”, “SOC code occupational health and safety specialist”, etc.

  • NAICS industry terms: “NAICS outpatient care centers”, “NAICS offices of other health practitioners”, “NAICS fitness and recreational sports centers”, “NAICS other personal care services”.

Even just adding “SOC” or “NAICS” to a query will surface more economic research and datasets.

Additions to Query B (economics / market research)

1) Add the core outcome variables economists use

When you search, include these terms to pull peer-reviewed work and credible reports:

  • “labor market”, “employment projection”, “wage”, “earnings”, “compensation”, “job postings”

  • “utilization”, “take-up”, “adherence”, “program evaluation”

  • “ROI”, “cost-effectiveness”, “medical spending”, “claims”, “absenteeism”, “presenteeism”

  • “work-related musculoskeletal disorders” + “productivity loss” + “workers’ compensation”

2) Add the “measurement/data” keywords that unlock real datasets

  • “ACS” (American Community Survey) + occupation

  • “OES/OEWS” (Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics)

  • “CPS” (Current Population Survey)

  • “NCS” (National Compensation Survey)

  • “MEPS” (Medical Expenditure Panel Survey) for spending outcomes

  • “NLSY” (National Longitudinal Survey of Youth) for life-course/labor outcomes

  • “job postings data” + (Burning Glass / Lightcast) + “wellness” / “ergonomics”

These terms often pull actual methods sections and dataset-based papers.

3) Add two “skeptical” terms so you capture the negative/neutral findings too

This prevents you from only seeing marketing claims:

  • “null effect”, “no significant effect”, “randomized trial”, “difference-in-differences”

  • “publication bias”, “heterogeneous effects”

4) Add the “buyer” segmentation terms (who pays)

For market sizing, you want papers/reports that distinguish:

  • “self-pay”, “cash-pay”, “reimbursement”, “covered benefit”

  • “employer-sponsored”, “health plan benefit”, “EAP”, “wellness vendor”

A few ready-to-run query strings (copy/paste)

Use these as seeds for your dynamic expansion:

  • "SOC code" ergonomics specialist wage

  • NAICS "offices of other health practitioners" massage therapist employment

  • ("work-related musculoskeletal disorders" OR WMSD) productivity cost "United States"

  • corporate wellness randomized trial absenteeism medical spending

  • breathwork facilitator labor market (will show you where research is thin)

  • ("human factors" OR ergonomics) consultant job postings United States

One more structural move that will pay off

Split Query A into two subtracks:

  • A1: “Licensed/regulated” track (LMT, PTA/OTA, Athletic Trainer, etc.)

  • A2: “Unlicensed/coaching” track (trainer, breathwork, mindfulness, somatics, energy work)

That separation makes it much easier to map where money comes from (insurance/employers vs self-pay) and what the credible scaling paths look like.

If you want, paste your current two query lists as you’ve got them so far (even if messy), and I’ll rewrite them into a clean “expanding keyword graph” format (titles → synonyms → settings → codes → datasets → outcomes).


You said:
Wow. Keep going.

ChatGPT said:

Cool — I’ll keep widening the net, but in a way that stays query-driven (so you can keep iterating without it turning into an unstructured list).

Upgrade the “career discovery” query into a proper job-title graph

Think of Query A as a graph with 4 layers:

A0) Core intent labels (what he is, in plain English)

Use these as “semantic anchors” that unlock titles employers actually use:

  • “musculoskeletal health” / “MSK health”

  • “pain management” (non-clinical)

  • “stress and recovery”

  • “movement + posture”

  • “occupational health”

  • “integrative health”

  • “somatics”

  • “mind-body” / “behavior change”

  • “ergonomics” / “human factors”

  • “return-to-work” / “work conditioning”

A1) Title clusters (the formal nouns recruiters use)

You already have most; here are additional high-overlap titles that tend to show up in real job postings:

Workplace / prevention

  • Occupational Health and Safety Specialist (ergonomics can be a specialty)

  • Industrial Ergonomist

  • Injury Prevention Coordinator

  • Workplace Health Specialist

  • Work Conditioning Specialist (often PT/OT-adjacent; sometimes coach-adjacent)

  • Return-to-Work Specialist

  • Disability Management Specialist

  • Workers’ Compensation Case Manager (may require RN/CRC depending on employer)

Fitness / corrective movement

  • Exercise Physiologist (more formal, but shows up in hospitals)

  • Wellness Coach (Corporate)

  • Movement Specialist

  • Mobility Specialist

  • Postural Restoration Specialist (brand-specific ecosystems exist)

  • Pilates Instructor (Rehab / Corrective)

  • Yoga Therapist (not psychotherapy; still “therapy” word—market uses it)

Mind-body / somatics

  • Somatic Practitioner

  • Somatic Coach

  • Breathwork Coach / Facilitator

  • Mindfulness Coach

  • Meditation Teacher

  • Stress Management Specialist

  • Trauma-Informed Coach (careful with claims; still common title language)

Care navigation / behavior change

  • Health Navigator / Patient Navigator

  • Health Educator

  • Care Coordinator

  • Wellness Program Coordinator

  • Population Health Coordinator

A2) Settings (where those titles actually live)

Add these “where clauses” to every title search:

  • “outpatient” / “ambulatory”

  • “sports medicine”

  • “occupational health clinic”

  • “work comp”

  • “EAP vendor”

  • “health plan vendor”

  • “university employee wellness”

  • “tech company ergonomics”

  • “manufacturing safety”

  • “warehouse injury prevention”

  • “concierge wellness”

  • “integrative medicine clinic”

  • “chiropractic office” (many “massage + corrective exercise” roles sit here)

A3) Credential and boundary terms (to filter the results cleanly)

These terms help you separate “can do hands-on care” vs “coaching” vs “regulated medicine”:

  • “scope of practice”

  • “state licensure”

  • “board certification”

  • “liability insurance”

  • “direct access”

  • “cash pay” / “self pay”

  • “CPT code” (signals reimbursement/clinical billing world)

  • “non-clinical” / “non-medical”

  • “health coaching” + “behavior change”

This layer is pure leverage because it makes your searches self-sorting.

Add two missing sector overlays that matter economically

Sector X) Digital health & MSK startups (tech-enabled care)

This is a big overlap with “programmer body” because these companies sell to employers and health plans.

Add titles:

  • Digital Health Coach

  • MSK Health Coach

  • Telehealth Health Coach

  • Clinical Operations Associate (sometimes non-licensed; system/people work)

  • Member Experience Specialist (health company role; heavy client communication)

Add sector terms:

  • “digital therapeutics”

  • “MSK digital health”

  • “virtual physical therapy”

  • “remote coaching platform”

  • “employer MSK benefit”

Sector Y) High-end “performance + longevity” (cash-pay premium)

This is where breathwork / somatics / nutrition / strength + recovery can be bundled.

Add titles:

  • Performance Coach (Health)

  • Recovery Coach

  • Longevity Coach

  • Concierge Wellness Coach

  • Lifestyle Medicine Coach (the physician “Lifestyle Medicine” board exists, but the market uses the phrase broadly)

Add sector terms:

  • “executive health”

  • “concierge medicine” (adjacent; careful on boundaries)

  • “longevity clinic”

Make Query B (economics research) behave like economists’ literature searches

Instead of “what’s the market,” economists ask five recurring questions. Build subqueries for each:

B1) Labor supply & wages

Keywords:

  • “wage dispersion”

  • “licensure wage premium”

  • “occupational licensing”

  • “self-employment earnings”

  • “gig work” + “wellness”

  • “returns to certification”

This is how you find research on whether licenses/certs actually change earnings and entry barriers.

B2) Demand & utilization

Keywords:

  • “healthcare utilization”

  • “complementary and integrative health use”

  • “patient preferences”

  • “cash-pay healthcare”

  • “price elasticity” + (massage, PT, wellness)

B3) Employer economics (the B2B money)

Keywords:

  • “workers’ compensation claims”

  • “absenteeism” / “presenteeism”

  • “productivity loss”

  • “return on investment”

  • “ergonomics intervention”

  • “injury prevention program evaluation”

B4) Program evaluation standards (to filter good evidence)

Keywords:

  • “randomized controlled trial”

  • “quasi-experimental”

  • “difference-in-differences”

  • “instrumental variables”

  • “regression discontinuity”

  • “heterogeneous treatment effects”

These terms force the search engine toward serious papers.

B5) Industry structure & business formation

Keywords:

  • “small business formation”

  • “self-employment”

  • “occupational entry”

  • “regulation”

  • “NAICS” + (fitness, personal care, other health practitioners)

This pulls work on how these markets behave as small service businesses (which is crucial for your friend).

A “dynamic expansion” trick that will keep working as you iterate

When you find one promising title or paper, immediately branch with these expansions:

Expansion set 1: synonym harvesting

Add:

  • “also known as”

  • “job description”

  • “competencies”

  • “career ladder”

  • “salary” / “wage”

  • “requirements”

Expansion set 2: classification harvesting (for datasets)

Add:

  • “SOC code”

  • “NAICS code”

  • “O*NET” (this is huge for skill/interest mapping)

  • “CIP code” (if you want degree programs later)

Expansion set 3: buyer harvesting (who pays)

Add:

  • “reimbursement”

  • “covered benefit”

  • “employer-sponsored”

  • “self-pay”

  • “subscription”

  • “package pricing”

That last set is often the difference between “cool role” and “economically viable role.”

More career titles that are surprisingly adjacent (and often overlooked)

These aren’t perfect matches, but they overlap strongly with “work with people, improve bodies/minds, not psychotherapy”:

  • Recreation Therapist (usually degree/licensure-ish depending on setting; still worth tracking)

  • Health Coach Supervisor / Program Lead (if he builds systems, not just 1:1)

  • Training and Development Specialist (Wellness / Safety) (corporate instruction role)

  • Employee Experience Specialist (Wellness focus) (HR-adjacent)

  • Customer Success Manager (Health / Wellness vendor) (high people contact; can be mission-aligned)

  • Wellness Retreat Facilitator (hospitality + instruction; very real market)

If you want this to keep compounding: a clean “two-query template” to evolve

Query A template (titles)

("ergonomics" OR "human factors" OR "injury prevention" OR "MSK health" OR "movement specialist" OR "somatic") AND ("coach" OR "specialist" OR "coordinator" OR "therapist" OR "trainer") AND ("corporate" OR "workplace" OR "outpatient" OR "integrative" OR "clinic")

Query B template (economics / evidence)

("occupational licensing" OR wages OR "employment projections" OR "program evaluation" OR "workers' compensation" OR absenteeism OR presenteeism OR "productivity loss") AND ("ergonomics" OR "corporate wellness" OR "massage therapy" OR "fitness trainer" OR "mindfulness") AND (United States)

If you tell me which direction you want the next iteration to emphasize—(1) quickest viable path, (2) highest long-term income ceiling, or (3) maximum alignment with energy work / somatics—I’ll push the query graph harder in that direction without dragging in irrelevant job families.


You said:
Tell me about the company Mindbody.

ChatGPT said:

Mindbody (often stylized MINDBODY) is a business-management software platform built for fitness, beauty, and wellness businesses— think yoga/Pilates studios, gyms, salons, spas, and similar appointment- and class-based operators. Its core value is “run the business + fill the schedule” in one stack: scheduling, client booking, payments, staff management, marketing, and customer communications. Mindbody+1

What it sells (in plain terms)

  • B2B SaaS for operators: an “all-in-one” system for managing classes/appointments and day-to-day operations. Mindbody+2Mindbody+2

  • Consumer discovery/booking layer: the Mindbody app helps consumers find and book experiences, which can feed demand back to businesses on the platform. Mindbody

Who uses it

Mindbody markets itself as powering tens of thousands of fitness/wellness businesses. Mindbody
(Exact counts vary by year/definition; their site gives directional scale and booking volume.)

Ownership and corporate structure

Mindbody was public (NASDAQ: MB) until it was taken private by Vista Equity Partners in a transaction valued at about $1.9B, announced Dec 24, 2018 and completed Feb 15, 2019. Mindbody+1

In 2025, Mindbody and ClassPass (plus Booker) were presented under a parent brand called Playlist. PR Newswire+2Athletech News+2

Major deals / timeline

  • 2019: Vista takes Mindbody private (~$1.9B). Mindbody+1

  • 2021: Mindbody announces it will acquire ClassPass (and a $500M strategic investment alongside the deal). Mindbody+2TechCrunch+2

  • June 2025: “Playlist” unveiled as the parent brand for Mindbody, Booker, and ClassPass. PR Newswire+1

  • Jan 2026: Reuters reports Playlist announced a merger with EGYM in a deal valued around $7.5B (including new equity financing), with Mindbody continuing to operate as a brand under Playlist. Reuters+1

(There was also reporting in Aug 2024 about IPO intentions for the Mindbody/ClassPass combined business at the time, but the 2026 merger news is the more current corporate-direction signal.) Reuters+1

Why Mindbody matters in the wellness economy (especially for your use case)

If your friend is thinking about building a practice (massage/bodywork, coaching, breathwork, etc.), Mindbody sits in the “infrastructure layer” of that world:

  • It’s a system of record for many boutique operators (scheduling + payments). Mindbody+1

  • It doubles as a marketplace/discovery funnel via the consumer app. Mindbody

  • As a result, a lot of studios and practitioners end up “speaking Mindbody” operationally (booking links, packages/memberships, cancellation policies, automated reminders, etc.).

If you want, tell me what you’re trying to learn about it—e.g., “Is Mindbody good for solo practitioners?”, “How does it compare to Vagaro/Square/Acuity?”, or “What’s the business model and pricing?”—and I’ll zoom straight into that slice.


You said:
Mindbody sucks, actually. I want to do whatever the opposite of Mindbody is. I feel like a lot of their clients just scam customers for overpriced "treatments" that just feel good after you do them.

ChatGPT said:

Yeah, I get the complaint. A lot of the “wellness marketplace” model (Mindbody-ish platforms + boutique studios) pushes businesses toward selling vibes, packages, and identity, not necessarily delivering verifiable outcomes. If you want “the opposite of Mindbody,” you can define it pretty cleanly.

What “opposite of Mindbody” looks like in practice

1) Outcomes-first, not experience-first

Mindbody-adjacent: optimize for retention, upsells, bundles, memberships, “treatments.”
Opposite: optimize for measurable change and honest uncertainty.

Concrete markers:

  • A baseline + reassessment cadence (pain scale, ROM measures, sleep, function tests, desk-tolerance, etc.)

  • “Here’s what we can reasonably expect” instead of “this will heal you”

  • Clear stop rules: “If you’re not improving by X, we refer out”

2) Transparent claims and scope

A lot of scamminess comes from claim inflation (implying medical treatment without being medical) or story-based causality (“trauma stored in your psoas,” etc.) marketed as fact.

Opposite means:

  • No diagnosis language if you’re not licensed to diagnose

  • No “medical-sounding” promises without evidence

  • A written scope: education + coaching + bodywork for comfort, with clear referral boundaries

3) Pricing that doesn’t require “belief”

The “overpriced treatment” vibe usually correlates with:

  • mysterious proprietary methods

  • multi-session packages sold up front

  • no ability to tell whether it worked besides “felt good”

Opposite pricing patterns:

  • pay-per-session by default

  • optional packages only after demonstrated benefit

  • sliding scale / community slots (if values-aligned)

  • published rates + what you actually get (minutes hands-on, assessment, home plan, follow-up)

4) Incentives aligned with “get better and leave”

A real opposite stance is: your business model can survive even if people graduate.

Ways people do that:

  • short, goal-bounded engagements (4–8 sessions)

  • group classes for maintenance (cheap per person)

  • periodic check-ins (quarterly tune-ups)

  • workplace contracts for prevention (so revenue isn’t tied to keeping individuals dependent)

5) “Feels good” is allowed—just not mislabeled

Feeling good after a session isn’t fake; it’s just not the same thing as:

  • curing a condition

  • remodeling tissue in a specific way

  • resolving trauma memories

Opposite framing:

  • “This is for relaxation, downshifting, comfort, and body awareness” (legit!)

  • “This is rehab/treatment for X condition” only if licensed and operating in that lane

If your friend is the one building this: what roles fit the “opposite” stance?

Not a full list—just the ones that naturally support this value system:

  • Ergonomics Consultant / Human Factors–style practitioner (prevention + measurable workstation changes)

  • Strength & conditioning / corrective exercise coach with tracking and reassessment

  • Licensed Massage Therapist who markets honestly as comfort/recovery + collaborates with PT/MD when needed

  • Health Educator / Wellness Program Coordinator who runs evidence-aware programming without miracle claims

(He can still include breathwork/meditation/somatics/energy work—just marketed as skills and experiences, not medical fixes.)

The “scam smell” checklist (useful as a filter)

If you want a quick heuristic for what tends to go wrong in that ecosystem:

  • Heavy reliance on packages, “limited-time” offers, memberships to access basic care

  • Proprietary jargon that can’t be questioned

  • Claims that conveniently require indefinite continuation

  • “Detox,” “alignment,” “balancing,” “energy block” presented as literal physiology

  • No referrals out, no collaboration, no reassessment—just more sessions

The opposite is basically: humble claims + measurement + clear scope + clean incentives.

If you want, I can translate this into a one-page “practice manifesto” your friend could actually use for branding and client screening (without any therapy-speak).


You said:
I want a career wherein the focus is *teaching* the client about how their own body works, rather than merely providing an experience where they feel good afterward.

ChatGPT said:

Got it. You’re describing an education-first body career: the product is client understanding + skill acquisition, and “feels good afterward” is incidental, not the point.

Here are the career titles that most naturally center teaching people how their body works, plus how each typically “packages” that teaching.

Best-fit career titles for an education-first body practice

1) Feldenkrais Practitioner

  • Core is guided learning (“Awareness Through Movement” lessons + individualized sessions).

  • The whole modality is framed as nervous-system learning and self-organization, not “treatment.”

2) Alexander Technique Teacher

  • Very explicitly pedagogical: habit change, coordination, posture/effort, and “use” in real activities.

  • Strong fit for desk/tech workers because it targets the how of movement, not just strengthening.

3) Somatic Movement Educator

  • This is a real job title in many contexts (schools, studios, private practice).

  • Often uses anatomy, attention training, and motor learning; can be rigorous or woo depending on the practitioner.

4) Clinical Exercise Physiologist (more school-y)

  • In hospitals/clinics, this role is fundamentally “teach physiology in action”: exercise prescriptions, monitoring, and education for chronic conditions.

  • Requires more formal training/certification than most coaching lanes.

5) Ergonomics Specialist / Ergonomics Consultant

  • The deliverable is education + systems change: how to set up work, how load accumulates, how to self-monitor and adjust.

  • Very “teach a mental model,” often with measurable before/after.

6) Corrective Exercise Specialist (usually via Personal Trainer base)

  • If done well, it’s a coaching job: assessment → explain → teach drills → reassess.

  • Works best when the coach commits to measurement and avoids medical claims.

7) Pilates Instructor (Rehab-informed / movement education style)

  • Pilates at its best is literally “learn to move”: breathing, spinal mechanics, scapula/hip control, graded loading.

  • There’s a big difference between “Pilates workout” and “Pilates as movement education.”

8) Yoga Instructor (anatomy-forward, function-forward)

  • Done in an education-first way, yoga can be a lab for proprioception, breathing mechanics, load tolerance, and attention.

  • The “opposite of feel-good” version is: clear models, constraints, progressions, and safety boundaries.

9) Health Educator / Health Education Specialist

  • More public-health oriented: teaching behavior change, stress, sleep, nutrition basics.

  • Less hands-on movement, more curriculum/program delivery.

10) Pain Science Educator / MSK Health Coach (usually a niche within coaching)

  • Not always a formal credentialed title, but this is a real market niche: explain pain, sensitivity, load, recovery, pacing.

  • Most credible when paired with trainer/ergonomics background and good referral networks.

“If you want the teaching to be the business model”

These formats push you away from “experience economy” traps:

  • 1:1 movement lessons (Feldenkrais/Alexander model)

  • Small-group cohorts (4–10 people) with a syllabus: “Desk Body 101,” “Back/Neck Mechanics,” etc.

  • Workplace workshops + follow-up office hours (ergonomics + movement)

  • Hybrid education + practice: teach a concept → guided drills → homework → reassess

Fastest on-ramp vs most “formally legit”

  • Fastest: Personal Trainer → corrective exercise / mobility → ergonomics consulting (you can build a very education-heavy practice quickly).

  • Most pedagogy-native: Feldenkrais / Alexander (training is longer, but the identity is “teacher,” not “healer”).

  • Most formal medical legitimacy: Exercise Physiologist / PTA/OTA track (more school, more regulation).

Quick filter: how to tell if a role supports your value

A role is aligned if the typical client outcome is:

  • “I understand what’s happening and can reproduce improvements on my own.”
    Not:

  • “I need to come back weekly to get fixed.”

If you want, tell me which direction you prefer:

  • (A) maximum rigor / less spiritual framing

  • (B) compatible with energy/somatics but still education-first

  • (C) workplace-facing (teaching teams, not individuals)

…and I’ll narrow this to the top 5 titles + the cleanest pathway for each.


You said:
Is there a market for any of these roles? What does the economics research say? I suspect the most profitable companies in this sector are ones that steer toward "experiences that feel good", rather than sessions where you learn something.

ChatGPT said:

Yes—there’s a real market, but it’s bifurcated:

  • Big, well-measured markets are the roles that sit inside BLS/SOC categories (massage, fitness, PT/OT assistants, occupational health & safety, etc.).

  • The education-first modalities you’re drawn to (Feldenkrais / Alexander / somatic education) exist, but they’re smaller, more self-employed, and harder to see in official labor statistics—which is itself an economics signal.

Below is what the economics / labor-market evidence supports, and how it relates to your suspicion.

1) The market is definitely there for the “adjacent-to-teaching” roles

These are explicitly tracked and show meaningful demand:

  • Massage Therapist: median $57,950 (May 2024) and +15% projected employment growth (2024–2034). Bureau of Labor Statistics+1

  • Fitness Trainers & Instructors: median $46,180 (May 2024). Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Physical Therapist Assistant: median $65,510 (May 2024). Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Occupational Therapy Assistant: median $68,340 (May 2024). Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Occupational Health & Safety Specialist (where ergonomics often lives institutionally): median $83,910 (May 2024). Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Athletic Trainer: median $60,250 (May 2024) and +11% projected growth (2024–2034). Bureau of Labor Statistics

Interpretation: there’s a strong market for body-adjacent work, including prevention and coaching. The higher-paying “teaching-heavy” institutional lane is usually workplace safety/occupational health/ergonomics, not boutique wellness. Bureau of Labor Statistics

2) Your “teaching people how their body works” modalities exist, but the market is smaller and structured differently

For Feldenkrais specifically, there’s published survey evidence suggesting a bimodal income reality: many practitioners earn a small share of income from it, while a minority earn most/all of their income from it (i.e., “side practice” vs “full practice”). PMC

Also: because these roles aren’t clean SOC categories, they often don’t show up in BLS wage tables the way massage/fitness do. That usually correlates with more self-employment + less standardized employer demand, and more reliance on direct-to-consumer marketing.

3) Your suspicion about “experiences that feel good” being more profitable is economically plausible

Economists have vocabulary for this:

  • Many wellness services behave like credence goods: customers often can’t evaluate true quality even after purchase, so sellers can lean on signals like branding, price, charisma, and immediate feelings. ScienceDirect+1

  • “Feeling better immediately” is a powerful signal in a credence/experience market, because it’s observable right away—whereas “I learned a durable model of my body and can self-correct” is real value but harder to notice and harder to attribute.

So yes: experience-first offerings can monetize more easily because they produce an immediate, emotionally salient outcome that supports repeat buying.

4) The research also suggests why “education-first” can be hard to sell inside corporations (but not impossible)

A landmark randomized trial of a multicomponent workplace wellness program found some improvements in self-reported behaviors, but no significant effects on clinical measures, healthcare spending/utilization, or employment outcomes after 18 months. JAMA Network+1

That result doesn’t mean “all education-first programs fail.” It means:

  • Cost-savings ROI claims are weak in rigorous evidence for many generic wellness programs.

  • Employer buyers may still pay for wellbeing for other reasons (recruiting/retention/culture), but the “economic proof” is often thinner than marketing suggests.

This tends to push B2B wellness toward engagement/experience (higher participation, happier employees) rather than hard outcomes.

5) Where economics points if you want “teaching-first” and strong money flows

If you want teaching to be central and you want to plug into markets with clearer budgets, the evidence above points to two relatively robust demand pools:

A) Workplace injury prevention / ergonomics / occupational health

  • More institutional spending logic; higher median pay in the formal job family. Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Teaching is built into the deliverable: training, workstation redesign, self-management practices.

B) Licensed lanes (or license-adjacent) where teaching is part of care

  • PTA/OTA/athletic training involve a lot of education and are paid in more standardized ways than boutique “movement education.” Bureau of Labor Statistics+2Bureau of Labor Statistics+2

6) A key economic lever you shouldn’t ignore: licensing tends to raise earnings (with tradeoffs)

Economics research repeatedly finds an occupational licensing wage premium (often single digits to ~15% depending on method/context), while also introducing barriers and reducing mobility. NBER+2American Economic Association+2

That matters because “education-first” work done under a recognized licensed umbrella (or adjacent to one) often has cleaner pricing power than fully unregulated niches.

Bottom line

  • Market exists for almost everything you named; the strongest “measurable” markets are the roles BLS tracks (massage/fitness/PTA/OTA/OH&S). Bureau of Labor Statistics+4Bureau of Labor Statistics+4Bureau of Labor Statistics+4

  • Your suspicion is economically coherent: experience/credence markets reward “feels good” because it’s the easiest quality signal. ScienceDirect+1

  • The “teaching-first” path can still be viable, but it tends to do best when it plugs into either (1) workplace prevention budgets or (2) licensed/standardized care lanes, rather than relying purely on boutique wellness demand. Bureau of Labor Statistics+1

If you want to keep building the two dynamic queries, the next high-leverage move is: decide whether you’re optimizing for (i) employer-funded prevention, (ii) cash-pay education practice, or (iii) hybrid (license anchor + education brand)—because the market evidence differs sharply across those.


You said:
Give me an entire lay of the land regarding licensing, and any of the aforementioned professions. Be thorough and complete.

ChatGPT said:

Below is the “licensing lay of the land” for the professions we’ve been talking about, with the key idea up front:

In the U.S., what matters isn’t just “do you have a credential?” — it’s (1) whether the state protects the title, (2) whether the state restricts the acts (what you do), and (3) whether you’re trying to bill insurance (which imposes its own rules).

I’ll organize this into:

  1. How licensing works (the meta-rules)

  2. A 4-tier map of the roles you named (from tightly licensed to mostly unregulated)

  3. Profession-by-profession: what’s licensed, what’s usually allowed, and where people accidentally cross the line

I’ll cite national sources where possible, and I’ll be explicit where things vary state-by-state.

1) The meta-rules of licensing in this space

Title protection vs practice restriction

  • Title protection: you can’t call yourself “Physical Therapist” unless licensed.

  • Practice restriction: even if you don’t use the title, you still can’t do certain things (e.g., treating a condition as physical therapy) if your state reserves those acts for a licensed profession.

Most states regulate healthcare with both.

Three different “permission systems” stack

  1. State professional licensure (legal right to practice and/or use a title).

  2. Facility/employer credentialing (hospital policies, HR requirements, vendor requirements).

  3. Payor rules (insurance/Medicare/Medicaid billing rules), which can be stricter than state law.

Example: You may be legally allowed to coach movement, but you can’t represent it as reimbursable PT unless you’re in that billing/credentialing lane.

The two big legal tripwires for education-first body work

  • Unlicensed practice of medicine (varies, but generally: diagnosing/treating disease or implying you do).

  • Unlicensed practice of a regulated profession (PT, OT, athletic training, dietetics, etc.), which is often defined in each profession’s practice act.

The clean strategy, if you want education-first work without a license, is:

  • market as education/coaching, not treatment,

  • avoid diagnosis language,

  • document client goals and learning outcomes (function/skill), not “treatment plans for conditions.”

2) The 4-tier map of the professions you named

Tier 1 — tightly licensed clinical professions (degree + national exam + state license)

These are the “hard boundary” roles:

  • Physical Therapist (PT) and Physical Therapist Assistant (PTA): licensure required; state boards regulate practice; PTA works under PT direction/supervision. American Physical Therapy Association+1

  • Occupational Therapist (OT) and Occupational Therapy Assistant (OTA/COTA): typically state licensure + national certification exam (NBCOT). AOTA+2nbcot.org+2

  • Athletic Trainer (AT): regulated in nearly all states; BOC exam is the widely recognized exam used by states. BOC Athletic Trainer Certification+1

  • Registered Dietitian Nutritionist (RDN/RD): national credential via CDR with accredited education + supervised practice + exam; many states license dietitians/nutritionists and/or restrict medical nutrition therapy. Commission on Dietetic Registration+2Commission on Dietetic Registration+2

Tier 2 — state licensed personal-care health-adjacent

  • Licensed Massage Therapist (LMT): most states regulate massage; typical requirements include approved education + passing an exam (often MBLEx). Bureau of Labor Statistics+2fsmtb.org+2

Tier 3 — not usually state-licensed, but often “credentialed” (employer/market-driven)

  • Ergonomics Specialist / Human Factors: generally not state-licensed; credibility often comes from degrees and/or professional certifications and employer requirements.

  • Occupational Health & Safety Specialist: not typically “licensed” by states like PT/OT; employers may prefer certifications/experience (and BLS tracks it as an occupation). Bureau of Labor Statistics

  • Health & Wellness Coach: not state-licensed in most places; there is an emerging national board-style credential (NBHWC) that is not a government license but can matter to employers. NBHWC+1

Tier 4 — largely unregulated modalities (private training norms, not state practice acts)

  • Feldenkrais Practitioner

  • Alexander Technique Teacher

  • Breathwork Facilitator

  • Meditation/Mindfulness Teacher

  • Tai Chi / Qigong Instructor

  • Reiki / energy work

  • “Somatic Practitioner / Somatic Coach”

  • Yoga teacher / Pilates instructor (with important caveats: sometimes regulated indirectly in clinical settings)

These can be legitimate, but the law cares about claims and actions, not your lineage.

3) Profession-by-profession licensing and scope

A) Physical Therapy Assistant (PTA)

Legal status: Licensed; must meet state requirements; licensure is required to work as a PTA. American Physical Therapy Association
Core boundary: PTA practice is by definition “assisting a PT” with direction/supervision frameworks (details vary by state and by payor). American Physical Therapy Association+1
What this means in practice:

  • You’re in a formal clinical system.

  • Documentation and billing are part of the job.

  • If someone wants “teach clients how their body works” inside regulated rehab, PTA is one of the cleanest ways to do that—at the cost of school/licensure.

B) Occupational Therapy Assistant (OTA/COTA)

Legal status: Typically state licensure and/or registration; national certification is through NBCOT; eligibility requires graduating from an accredited OTA program. nbcot.org+2AOTA+2
Core boundary: OT is about enabling function in daily life; education and habit/environment modification are central.
What this means in practice:

  • Strong “teaching people how they work in real life” vibe.

  • More “life systems” than “strength and mobility,” though there’s overlap.

C) Athletic Trainer (AT)

Legal status: Regulated in almost all states; legal recognition by the state is required to practice; BOC exam is recognized by state regulators. BOC Athletic Trainer Certification+1
Training/entry: Eligibility for certification typically runs through CAATE-accredited education + BOC exam. BOC Athletic Trainer Certification+1
What this means in practice:

  • More “sports/performance injury prevention & acute care” oriented, but increasingly broader settings (schools, clinics, occupational).

  • Very education-heavy (warmups, return-to-play, load management), but still licensed/regulatory.

D) Registered Dietitian Nutritionist (RDN/RD) vs “Nutrition Coach”

RDN/RD credential: Through the Commission on Dietetic Registration (CDR): accredited education + supervised practice + exam; as of Jan 1, 2024, a graduate degree is required for RD exam eligibility. Commission on Dietetic Registration+2Commission on Dietetic Registration+2
State law: Many states regulate “dietitian/nutritionist” titles and/or medical nutrition therapy—state specifics vary widely.

Nutrition coaching (non-RD):

  • Typically not state-licensed, but the big boundary is: don’t present yourself as treating medical conditions with nutrition unless you’re in a licensed lane your state recognizes.

  • You can usually teach general nutrition, behavior change, and performance-oriented eating.

E) Licensed Massage Therapist (LMT)

Legal status: Massage is regulated in most states; therapists typically need a state license or registration; requirements vary but usually include completing an approved program and passing an exam. Bureau of Labor Statistics+1
MBLEx: The Massage & Bodywork Licensing Examination is a common national licensing exam used by many regulated jurisdictions. fsmtb.org+2fsmtb.org+2
Boundary (big one):

  • Massage can be framed as comfort, recovery, stress reduction, and sometimes functional support.

  • The line is crossed when marketing or practice implies diagnosing/treating medical conditions in a way reserved for PT/OT/medicine by your state.

F) Personal Trainer / Strength Coach / Corrective Exercise Specialist

Legal status: Generally not state-licensed (in the way PT/OT are).
Practical reality: It’s a “market credential” world (certifications, insurance, employer standards).
Boundary:

  • Coaching movement and building strength is fine.

  • Diagnosing injuries, prescribing rehab for a medical condition, or representing services as “physical therapy” is where people get into trouble.

If you want education-first, this lane is powerful because you can structure everything as:

  • assessment → explanation → skill-building plan → reassessment
    without claiming to be a clinic.

G) Ergonomics / Human Factors / Occupational Health & Safety

Legal status: Typically not “licensed professions” in the same sense; more often governed by employer requirements and professional certifications.
Why it’s a clean education-first fit: the deliverable is explicitly teaching + environment design + behavior and workflow change.

This lane tends to avoid the “feel-good experience economy” trap because:

  • it’s often B2B,

  • outcomes can be operationalized (discomfort scores, workstation changes, injury rates, productivity proxies).

H) Health & Wellness Coach (including NBHWC)

Legal status: Not generally a government license; often self-regulated / employer-regulated.
NBHWC: A board-style credential requires an approved training program, a set of coaching sessions, and education or work-experience requirements. NBHWC+1
Boundary:

  • Coaching is typically framed as behavior change and education.

  • Avoid mental health treatment claims and medical treatment claims unless you also hold those licenses.

I) Feldenkrais / Alexander / Somatic Education / Breathwork / Mindfulness / Tai Chi / Qigong / Reiki

Legal status: Generally unlicensed; legitimacy comes from training quality, ethics, and how you describe your service.
Two key boundaries:

  1. Don’t present yourself as a licensed clinician unless you are.

  2. Don’t claim to treat/diagnose medical conditions unless you’re within a licensed scope.

Best practice for “education-first” positioning (legally and ethically):

  • “Lessons,” “training,” “education,” “self-management,” “skill,” “awareness,” “movement literacy”

  • Avoid “treat,” “cure,” “rehabilitate,” “medical,” and condition-specific promises.

4) The “complete list” of what tends to be regulated vs not (quick reference)

Usually state-licensed / tightly regulated

  • PT, PTA (state boards; supervision frameworks) American Physical Therapy Association+1

  • OT, OTA/COTA (state licensure + NBCOT certification pathway) AOTA+2nbcot.org+2

  • Athletic Trainer (regulated in nearly all states; BOC exam recognized) BOC Athletic Trainer Certification+1

  • Massage Therapy (most states regulate; MBLEx widely used) Bureau of Labor Statistics+2fsmtb.org+2

  • Dietitian Nutritionist (national credential; state regulation varies; grad degree requirement for exam eligibility as of 2024) Commission on Dietetic Registration+1

Usually not state-licensed (but can be credentialed/regulated by employers/contracts)

  • Personal Trainer / Strength Coach / Mobility Coach

  • Ergonomics Specialist / Human Factors roles

  • Health Coach (NBHWC-type credentials exist) NBHWC+1

  • Yoga/Pilates teacher (except in some clinical billing contexts)

Mostly unregulated modalities

  • Feldenkrais, Alexander, breathwork, mindfulness, tai chi/qigong, reiki/energy work, somatic coaching

5) The practical consequence: “education-first” is easiest in 3 lanes

If your priority is teaching people how their body works, and you want to stay clean on licensing:

  1. Ergonomics / workplace prevention (education is the product; rarely mistaken for medical practice)

  2. Training/coaching with explicit assessment + reassessment (movement literacy, strength, self-management)

  3. A pedagogy-native method (Feldenkrais / Alexander) marketed as lessons, not treatment

If you want, I can also do a Kansas City–specific version (Missouri + Kansas) that names the relevant boards and the most common licensing requirements in those two states—but the general structure above is how the whole U.S. landscape is shaped.

Entities referenced (for clarity in your head, not because you need to care)

U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, American Physical Therapy Association, National Board for Certification in Occupational Therapy, Board of Certification for the Athletic Trainer, Federation of State Massage Therapy Boards, National Board for Health & Wellness Coaching, Commission on Dietetic Registration


You said:
Do a Kansas City version. And then, for fun, do a Utah version.

ChatGPT said:

Here’s the Kansas City metro licensing lay-of-the-land (split Missouri side vs Kansas side) and then a Utah version. I’ll stick to the professions we’ve been discussing and call out the “gotchas” that matter for an education-first practice.

Kansas City metro

The big picture: KC is two regulatory worlds

  • On the Missouri side, a bunch of these roles are regulated at the state level through the Missouri Division of Professional Registration and its boards (massage, PT/PTA, OT/OTA, etc.). Missouri Department of Public Safety+2Missouri Department of Public Safety+2

  • On the Kansas side, most clinical roles are state-licensed via the Kansas State Board of Healing Arts, but massage therapy is a special case: Kansas historically has not had statewide massage licensure, and regulation is often local (city/county). KLRD+1

That means “what you can call yourself” and “what paperwork you need” can change dramatically depending on whether you’re in Kansas City proper, Johnson County suburbs, etc.

A) Missouri side (KC, MO and nearby MO suburbs)

1) Massage Therapy

Status: State regulated; license issued through the Missouri Board of Therapeutic Massage. Missouri Department of Public Safety+2Missouri Department of Public Safety+2
Typical components you should expect (high-level):

  • Education from an approved program (commonly ~500 hours is discussed in Missouri-facing requirement summaries) and a national exam (often MBLEx), plus a criminal background check/fingerprints. MBLEx Guide+3Missouri Department of Public Safety+3Missouri Department of Public Safety+3

Why this matters for an education-first practice: LMT licensure gives you a mainstream “anchor,” and you can still run sessions as “education + self-care skills + bodywork,” as long as you don’t drift into regulated “diagnosis/treatment” claims outside your scope.

2) Physical Therapy Assistant (PTA)

Status: Licensed through the Missouri “healing arts” pathway (application materials run through the Missouri board system). pr.missouri.gov+2pr.missouri.gov+2
Key boundary: PTA work is tied to the physical therapy system (supervision/direction rules and formal scope). In practice, it’s the cleanest “teach clients how their body works” role inside clinical rehab, but it’s not the least-school option.

3) Occupational Therapy Assistant (OTA)

Status: Licensed/regulated through the Missouri Board of Occupational Therapy. Missouri Department of Public Safety+2Missouri Department of Public Safety+2
Key boundary: “assist under OT supervision” is central; documentation, scope, and setting requirements are real.

4) (Sports/clinic) Athletic Training

Missouri regulates athletic training as well (and settings often require the national BOC credential even when state rules vary). If your friend is drawn to teaching load management and injury prevention, AT is a strong fit—but usually implies more schooling than massage/training.

B) Kansas side (Johnson County suburbs, KCK, etc.)

1) Massage therapy (important and weird in Kansas)

Status: Kansas has not historically required statewide massage licensure, so people can legally practice massage in Kansas without state fees/standards/oversight as long as they don’t present themselves as a physician/healer or as a PT (Kansas statutes carve-outs are discussed in a Kansas Legislative Research memo). KLRD

But: Kansas cities can (and do) regulate massage locally (permits, minimum hours, CPR/First Aid, etc.). The Kansas Legislative Research Department memo explicitly describes local oversight and gives examples. KLRD

Two concrete examples of local licensing you’ll run into in the KC-area ecosystem:

  • Prairie Village requires a city massage therapist license; their published requirements include things like proof of 500 hours (or NCBTMB certification), CPR/First Aid, and annual renewal. Prairie Village

  • The Kansas Legislative Research memo also points to the Mission municipal code as an example of city permits for therapists/establishments. KLRD

Legislative movement: As of Jan 30, 2026, there’s an active push again for statewide Kansas licensure (e.g., SB 253 advocacy), so this landscape may change. American Massage Therapy Association

Practical takeaway for Kansas-side KC: If you’re practicing massage anywhere in Johnson County, you must check the city you’re in (Overland Park / Olathe / Lenexa / Mission / Prairie Village / etc.) for local massage rules, business licensing, zoning, and permit requirements.

2) PT/PTA, OT/OTA, Athletic Trainer (Kansas)

These are cleanly state-regulated through Kansas State Board of Healing Arts.

  • PTA: Kansas has an explicit PTA certification category. ksbha.ks.gov+1

  • OTA: Kansas licenses OTAs and defines the role as assisting under OT supervision. ksbha.ks.gov+1

  • Athletic Trainer: Kansas licenses ATs and defines athletic training practice; Kansas also requires a “practice protocol” in at least some contexts (it’s referenced in Kansas sources). ksbha.ks.gov+1

C) Roles that are not state-licensed (but still matter in KC)

These generally don’t require a Kansas or Missouri state license, but they may require:

  • certifications (employer preference),

  • insurance,

  • and very careful marketing language (to avoid implying you’re practicing a licensed profession).

Most relevant:

  • Personal Trainer / Strength Coach / Corrective Exercise style roles

  • Ergonomics / Human Factors / Workplace injury prevention consulting

  • Health coaching (market credentials; not usually a state license)

  • Feldenkrais / Alexander / mindfulness / breathwork / tai chi / qigong / reiki (mostly unregulated modalities)

For your education-first goal, ergonomics and trainer + assessment + reassessment models are often the cleanest “teach the model” lanes without stepping into PT/OT.

Utah version

Utah is much more “state-licensed” for the clinical and massage-adjacent categories, all under the Utah Division of Professional Licensing.

1) Massage Therapy (Utah)

Status: State-licensed through Utah DOPL. commerce.utah.gov+2commerce.utah.gov+2
Notable Utah-specific features:

  • Utah requires exam pathways and has had evolving classifications (including assistant/apprentice classifications and law/rule exam elements, depending on classification and rules at the time). commerce.utah.gov+1

  • The DOPL “apply” page for Massage Therapist lists education/apprenticeship verification and exam-result submission. commerce.utah.gov

2) Physical Therapist Assistant (Utah)

Status: Licensed via Utah DOPL (there is a dedicated “apply for PTA license” page). commerce.utah.gov+1

3) Occupational Therapy Assistant (Utah)

Status: Licensed via Utah DOPL (dedicated application page). commerce.utah.gov+1

4) Athletic Trainer (Utah)

Status: Licensed via Utah DOPL; the application page references needing transcripts and BOC certification documentation. commerce.utah.gov+1
Utah statutes also explicitly state that a license is required to engage in athletic training practice (with exceptions). Utah Legislature

The practical “do this first” checklist (KC and Utah)

If you’re doing this as a real plan (not just curiosity), the fastest way to avoid surprises is:

  1. Decide which “lane” you’re in: massage (hands-on) vs clinical assistant (PTA/OTA) vs workplace ergonomics vs trainer/coaching.

  2. If it’s Kansas-side massage, identify the exact city and check city clerk / business licensing (Prairie Village style pages are the pattern). Prairie Village+1

  3. For Utah and Missouri (and Kansas clinical roles), start on the state board/DOPL application pages because they’re the source of truth for required documents and current rules. Missouri Department of Public Safety+2commerce.utah.gov+2

If you tell me which specific titles you want to prioritize (e.g., LMT vs ergonomics vs trainer vs Feldenkrais/Alexander), I can write a “minimal path” for each one in KC and in Utah that focuses only on the required steps and the highest-risk boundary issues.


You said:
Do a deep dive into Kansas / Johnson County specifically, here.

ChatGPT said:

I’ll web-browse Johnson County and key city ordinances/licensing pages (Overland Park, Olathe, Lenexa, Shawnee, Mission, Prairie Village, Leawood, etc.), then summarize what’s required for massage/bodywork and adjacent wellness practices, plus the practical “do/don’t” boundaries.

Here’s the Kansas / Johnson County “lay of the land” as it relates to the roles you’ve been circling (hands-on bodywork, movement education, ergonomics, breathwork, “mind–body” coaching, etc.). I’m going to be blunt about the licensing fault lines, because that’s where people accidentally drift into trouble.

The core reality in Kansas

1) Kansas has no statewide massage license

Kansas is one of the states where massage therapy is largely regulated at the city level, not the state level—so the rules that matter in Johnson County are usually municipal ordinances (and sometimes zoning/home-occupation rules), not a Kansas state board. This is a big reason the KC-area landscape feels fragmented. CE Massage+1

2) Johnson County is the “high-regulation” pocket

Even though Kansas doesn’t license massage statewide, several Johnson County cities essentially implement a “mini-state-license” model: ~500 hours education, CPR/First Aid, often a national exam (MBLEx/NCBTMB), background checks, and business/establishment licensing.

Johnson County deep dive: massage/bodywork licensing by city

Below are the cities that come up constantly in practice because they’re major job markets and/or have explicit therapist licensing.

Overland Park

Overland Park’s published requirements include 500 hours via certified transcript (with specific curriculum subjects listed), plus First Aid + CPR, photos, etc. City of Overland Park, Kansas
This is one of the stricter Kansas cities and often treated as the “reference model.”

Lenexa

Lenexa requires a massage therapist license (and separately, a massage establishment license if you run a location). Their business-license page explicitly lists 500 hours transcripts and hands-on CPR/First Aid, and notes 12 hours of continuing education for renewal. lenexa.com

Olathe

Olathe regulates both massage therapists and establishments; their ordinance packet shows that (a) the city runs a criminal background investigation, and (b) therapists must meet education requirements and, if seeking a therapist license, be NCBTMB-certified or have passed the MBLEx. olatheks.legistar.com

Shawnee

Shawnee’s licensing page is unusually clear: therapist application requires 500 hours plus proof of passing NCBTMB exam or MBLEx, plus CPR/First Aid; the therapist fee listed is $25/year. It also describes establishment licensing fees and an “off-premises” concept. cityofshawnee.org+1

Leawood

Leawood’s code spells out educational requirements: 500 hours from approved schools, proof via certified transcripts, and details on how instruction can be delivered (hands-on vs theory) in parts of the ordinance. Leawood
Leawood’s licensing page also indicates you may need official transcripts emailed or an NCBTMB certificate depending on applicant status/history. Leawood

Prairie Village

Prairie Village explicitly lists: 500 hours transcript OR NCBTMB certification, plus First Aid (and other application items). Prairie Village

Mission

Mission requires a city permit to perform massage therapy. The code also shows a home-occupation pathway (massage may be done at a private residence if approved as a home occupation) and includes a continuing-education provision (example: 12 hours CE in the prior 12 months for licensed therapists). eCode360+1

Merriam

Merriam is another city that publicly lists multi-part requirements (written application, age verification, transcripts, CPR/First Aid, etc.). merriam.org+1

Roeland Park

Roeland Park’s application document references education requirements in its city code and explicitly mentions CPR/First Aid as part of what you submit. roelandpark.net+1

Pattern you should assume across Johnson County, even when details vary:
If you want to do paid hands-on massage/bodywork, many Johnson County cities will want:

  • ~500 hours massage education (documented via transcript)

  • CPR + First Aid

  • Often MBLEx or NCBTMB

  • City permit/license + sometimes establishment license

  • Often zoning/home-occupation compliance for home-based practice
    (You can see that bundle clearly in Lenexa and Shawnee, for example.) lenexa.com+1

The “other licensing cliff edges” for the rest of your friend’s interests

Your friend’s intended work (teaching clients about their bodies, movement, habits, and “why”) sits in a wide zone that can be done ethically—but certain titles and activities are tightly regulated.

Hands-on rehab: PT/OT is not “optional-licensing”

  • Kansas State Board of Healing Arts licenses Physical Therapists and other healing-arts professions. ksbha.ks.gov+1

  • This is the bright line: if you market or practice as PT/OT, or drift into what regulators treat as PT/OT practice, that’s a licensing issue.

Counseling/therapy

  • Kansas Behavioral Sciences Regulatory Board sets degree-heavy requirements for counseling licenses (e.g., LPC requires a master’s and other steps). ksbsrb.ks.gov
    So the “below therapy” zone is real, but the moment it becomes treatment of mental disorders (or you present yourself as such), you collide with regulated practice.

Nutrition

  • In Kansas, dietitian practice/title is licensed through Kansas Department for Aging and Disability Services / Health Occupations Credentialing. kdads.ks.gov

  • Kansas also passed Dietitian Licensure Compact legislation in 2025 (relevant if your friend ever wants multi-state portability). Eat Right Pro
    Practical takeaway: “nutrition coaching” can be a thing; “dietitian/nutritionist” claims can become title/practice-regulated depending on how it’s represented and what’s being treated.

Acupuncture / “energy work”

If the practice is actually acupuncture (needles), Kansas routes this through the Kansas State Board of Healing Arts. ksbha.ks.gov+1
If it’s non-medical “energy work” without regulated claims, it’s typically not licensed—but the risk vector becomes false/medical claims, not licensure.

The “opposite of Mindbody” in Johnson County: where teaching-focused work fits cleanly

If the guiding principle is “teach the client how their body works” rather than selling feel-good experiences, the cleanest lanes (licensing-wise) usually look like:

  1. Movement educator / corrective exercise / mobility coach (non-licensed; avoid rehab claims)

  2. Ergonomics coach / workstation & habits educator (often non-licensed; can be very evidence-oriented)

  3. Breathwork instructor (non-licensed; avoid treating respiratory/mental disorders)

  4. Strength & conditioning coach / personal trainer (credentialed but not state-licensed; stay out of “treat injury” framing)

  5. Massage therapist (city-licensed in many JoCo cities) with an education-forward model (teaching anatomy, self-care, loading principles, etc., while still providing hands-on work)

The only one in that list that’s routinely permit/license-gated in Johnson County cities is massage therapy. Everything else is mainly governed by marketing claims + scope boundaries, not a licensing board—unless you cross into dietetics, psychotherapy, or physical therapy.

One KC-metro complication worth stating explicitly

Because Kansas City is split by the state line: if your friend ever plans to work on the Missouri side, Missouri does have a statewide massage regulator (Missouri Board of Therapeutic Massage), so the licensing picture changes immediately when you cross over. Missouri Department of Public Safety

If you want, I can do the next-level Johnson County cut in two directions (without adding fluff):

  1. “If he wants hands-on bodywork” → a step-by-step compliance map per city (therapist vs establishment vs home occupation vs off-premises).

  2. “If he wants teaching-only” → exactly what language/offer structures tend to keep people safely on the education side of the line (while still being serious, non-scammy, and technical).


You said:
Narrow in on "healing-arts". Tell me more about licensure, economics, and the lay of the land of some of these other professions in Kansas, specifically around (or inside) the Johnson County area.

ChatGPT said:

Here’s how the “healing-arts” landscape works in Kansas, with a practical focus on **Johnson County / Overland Park and the nearby Kansas City metro.

1) First: what Kansas means by “healing arts” (and what triggers licensure)

The core idea

Kansas draws a hard line between:

  • Licensed clinical practice (diagnosis/treatment of conditions; regulated titles/scope), and

  • General wellness / education / coaching (often unlicensed, if you don’t cross into diagnosis/treatment or protected titles).

Under the Kansas Healing Arts Act definition, “healing arts” is broad—covering systems/treatments/diagnosis/prescription/etc. kgi.contentdm.oclc.org
And Kansas makes it explicitly unlawful to practice the healing arts without the appropriate license (with some statutory exceptions). Kansas Revisor of Statutes

What this means in practice (high-signal boundary markers)

You’re generally in licensed territory if you do things like:

  • advertise or imply you treat, diagnose, rehabilitate, cure, or manage a condition as a clinician,

  • use protected titles (PT, OT, DC, L.Ac., PA, etc.),

  • do hands-on techniques that are defined as part of a regulated clinical scope (PT/OT/AT/etc.), or

  • represent yourself as part of a regulated profession’s services.

You’re generally in unlicensed territory if you:

  • stay in education, habit change, fitness, general stress reduction, movement instruction, etc.,

  • avoid protected titles,

  • avoid diagnosing/treating named conditions,

  • and communicate clearly that it’s not medical care.

That boundary is the business model choice you were hinting at: “teach clients how their body works” can be positioned as education—but some modalities (manual therapy under PT/OT, acupuncture, chiropractic) become licensure-driven very quickly.

2) Who regulates “healing arts” in Kansas (the main board)

Most of the core “healing arts” professions you’re talking about are regulated by the Kansas State Board of Healing Arts. Their professions list includes: Acupuncturist, Athletic Trainer, Chiropractic, MD/DO, Naturopathic Doctor, Podiatry, CNM-I, OT/OTA, PT/PTA, PA, Radiologic Technologist, Respiratory Therapist, Contact Lens Distributor. KS Board of Healing Arts

For your “healing-arts” focus, the most relevant items in that list are usually:

  • Physical Therapist / Physical Therapist Assistant

  • Occupational Therapist / Occupational Therapy Assistant

  • Athletic Trainer

  • Licensed Acupuncturist

  • Doctor of Chiropractic

  • Physician Assistant (more medical/clinical than your friend likely wants)
